name: Prose: "His First Flight" — Liam O'Flaherty

sourceRefs.label: 2022_q page 8 Q26 (confirms the lesson is about a Seagull, with answer key)
civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated MCQ set (6 questions); andedge.com/tnpscgk.in - plot summary

researchNotes: First of the 29 Unit VII PROSE pieces to be authored — Unit VII's 30 poems are now complete except 'The River' (see literature-evidence-stash.md). OCR grep for 'his first flight/o.flaherty' found exactly 1 real hit, but it's a strong confirmatory one (confirms the subject is a seagull). Note: the same OCR page (2022_q page 8) also carried 3 unrelated questions from OTHER passages bundled on the same page (The Last Lesson's character-matching; The Tempest's 'what game did Ferdinand play' detail; an unrelated Dutch-boy-style passage not in our syllabus) — the Last Lesson and Tempest ones were stashed in literature-evidence-stash.md for their own future topics rather than mixed in here. Expanded 2026-07 pass: added the story's broader metaphorical reading (dependence→independence, the ledge vs. flight symbolism), O'Flaherty's immersive descriptive technique, and its central paradox (fear of falling vs. falling being what teaches flight).

reading.intro: A short story about a young seagull too afraid to fly from his cliff ledge, alone after his siblings have already flown, who is finally driven to attempt flight by hunger and his mother's coaxing — and discovers joy and confidence once he succeeds.

qa.question: Who wrote "His First Flight", and what is the story about?
qa.answer: Liam O'Flaherty (1896-1984), an Irish novelist and short-story writer. The story follows a young seagull, left alone on a high ledge after his two brothers and one sister have already flown off, who is too afraid to attempt his own first flight.

qa.question: Why was the young seagull afraid to fly?
qa.answer: He was terrified at the thought of his wings being unable to support him over the vast, far-below expanse of sea — not simply a generic fear of heights, but specifically the thought of his wings failing him mid-flight.

qa.question: How did the young seagull's parents try to get him to fly, and what finally triggered his first flight attempt?
qa.answer: His parents largely ignored/left him alone for a day rather than coaxing him directly. It was ultimately his MOTHER's attempt to bring him food (tempting him toward the edge) that finally prompted his first flight.

qa.question: What happened when the young seagull tried to land on the sea after his first flight, and how did his family react?
qa.answer: He discovered, to his relief, that he could float on the water's surface rather than sinking — a moment of fear giving way to a new, safe discovery. His family then praised him and offered him food, celebrating his success.

qa.question: What emotion did the young seagull feel once he successfully completed his first flight?
qa.answer: Joy — despite his initial terror, his first successful flight left him fearless, exhilarated, and confident, flapping his wings to soar with a joyous scream.

qa.question: What broader theme does the story represent, beyond a literal bird's first flight?
qa.answer: A metaphor for human growth from dependence to independence — the transition from childhood security into self-reliant adulthood, where confronting fear is a necessary part of growing up.

qa.question: What do the 'ledge' and 'flight' each symbolize in the story?
qa.answer: The ledge symbolizes safety, but also stagnation and limitation. Flight symbolizes freedom, growth, and self-discovery — the story frames clinging to safety as ultimately more limiting than the frightening leap into growth.

qa.question: What literary technique does O'Flaherty use to immerse the reader in the seagull's experience?
qa.answer: Vivid, sensory description of the sea, sky, and the seagull's own physical sensations and movements — keeping the narrative tightly anchored to the bird's subjective experience of fear and eventual joy, rather than an outside observer's viewpoint.

qa.question: What central paradox does the story present about fear and growth?
qa.answer: The young seagull fears falling — but it is precisely his desperate lunge (which feels like falling) that teaches him to fly. The story suggests growth requires risking the very failure one is most afraid of.

questions.prompt: "His First Flight" is a lesson about a:
Why was the young seagull afraid to fly?
options: He had never seen anyone else fly
He thought his wings would not support him
He did not want to leave his ledge
He was scared of other birds
questions.explanation: He thought his wings would not support him (fear of unsupported wings).

questions.prompt: How did the young seagull's parents try to encourage him to fly?
options: They ignored him for a day
They brought him food every hour
They flew away and left him alone permanently
questions.explanation: They ignored him for a day.

questions.prompt: What finally prompted the young seagull to make his first flight?
options: His mother's attempt to bring food
The sight of his siblings flying
questions.explanation: His mother's attempt to bring food.

questions.prompt: What emotion did the young seagull feel after his first successful flight?
What happened when the young seagull tried to land on the sea?
options: He found he could float on the water
He got scared and flew back to the ledge
He was pulled under by the current
questions.explanation: He found he could float on the water.

questions.prompt: How did the seagull's family react to his first flight?
options: They scolded him for being late
They praised him and offered him food
They left him alone on the cliff
questions.explanation: They praised him and offered him food.

options: Human growth from dependence to independence
questions.explanation: Human growth from dependence to independence.

questions.prompt: In the story's symbolism, the 'ledge' represents:
The story's central paradox is that:
options: The seagull never learns to fly
The seagull's fearful 'fall' is exactly what teaches him to fly
His parents actively taught him to fly
Flying turns out to be easy from the start
questions.explanation: His fearful lunge (feeling like a fall) is what teaches him to fly.
